lessons.title: Seeing Your Network: Your Address, Your Way Out, and a Reachability Test
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When your phone joins Wi-Fi, there's a settings screen showing your network name, your IP address, and the router. Linux has the same information, but you ask for it by typing commands. Three questions cover almost everything a beginner needs at first: *What is my address? Which way do I get out? Can I actually reach that other machine?*

**Build.** First, some plain words. A **network** is just machines that can talk to each other. An **IP address** is the number that identifies one machine on a network, the way a street address identifies one house (for example `192.168.1.42`). An **interface** is one network connection on your machine, usually a card or chip; common names are `eth0` (a wired connection) and `wlan0` (Wi-Fi). The **gateway** (also called the *default route*) is the device, normally your home router, that passes your traffic onward when you want to reach anything outside your own local network. Picture a building: your office has a room number (your IP), and the mailroom by the exit (the gateway) is where anything addressed to the outside world goes.

The one command for addresses and routes is `ip`. Ask it what your address is, and which way out it uses:

```bash
ip addr show     # your interfaces and their IP addresses (short form: ip a)
ip route show    # your routes, including the default gateway
```

In `ip addr`, the line starting `inet` shows your IP address. In `ip route`, the line starting `default via` shows your gateway. That's your address and your way out.

Now, *can you reach a machine?* The classic test is `ping`. It sends a tiny "are you there?" message and waits for the echo back, timing the round trip:

```bash
ping -c3 8.8.8.8     # send 3 probes to a host, then stop
```

If replies come back, the host is **reachable**. If nothing comes back, it is unreachable: it may be off, the address may be wrong, or something in between may be blocking you. The `-c3` just means "send three and stop" so it doesn't run forever.

One note for later: the modern command is `ip` (from a toolkit called *iproute2*). You may see an older command called `ifconfig` in old tutorials; it is outdated and often not even installed now. Reach for `ip`. The deep theory of *how* addresses and packets work (TCP/IP and the rest) lives in the separate Networking topic; here we just learn the Linux tools to see and control it.

**Recap.** `ip addr` shows your address, `ip route` shows your gateway (your way out), and `ping` tests whether you can reach another machine. Three commands, three everyday questions answered.

items.prompt: Which command shows your machine's own IP address?
items.explanation: `ip addr show` lists your interfaces and the IP addresses assigned to them (look for the line starting `inet`). `ip route show` tells you the way out (the gateway), `ping` tests reachability, and `ss` shows connections and listening ports -- none of those is where you read your own address.

items.prompt: What is the gateway (the "default route") on a typical home machine?
choices: The website you are currently visiting
The router that forwards your traffic toward anything outside your local network
The DNS server that turns names into numbers
items.explanation: The gateway is the device (usually your home router) that your machine hands off traffic to when the destination is not on your own local network -- like the mailroom by the building exit. It is not your own address, not the remote website, and not the name-lookup service (that is DNS, covered later).

items.prompt: You want to check whether a server is reachable from your machine. Which command is the simple first test?
items.explanation: `ping` sends a small "are you there?" probe and waits for the echo reply, which is the quickest way to ask "can I reach this host?" `ip link` and `ip addr` describe your own machine, and `nft list ruleset` shows firewall rules -- none of those tests reachability to a remote host.

items.prompt: In the output of `ip addr show`, which part is your machine's IP address?
items.explanation: The IP address appears on the line beginning with `inet` (for example `inet 192.168.1.42/24`). `eth0` is the interface name, `link/ether ...` is the hardware (MAC) address, and `UP` just means the interface is active.

items.prompt: An IP address is best described as which of the following?
choices: The brand name of the network card
A number that identifies one machine on a network, like a house's street address
The password needed to join the Wi-Fi
The speed of the connection in megabits per second
items.explanation: An IP address identifies one machine on a network so traffic can find it, just as a street address identifies one house. It is not the hardware brand, not a Wi-Fi password, and not a measure of speed.

items.prompt: You ran `ping -c3 example.com` and got zero replies. Which conclusion is reasonable?
choices: The host is definitely powered on and healthy
Your own network card has been physically removed
The host may be down, the address may be wrong, or something may be blocking the probes
Your IP address must have changed to a public one
items.explanation: No replies means "not reachable right now," but ping cannot tell you exactly why: the target could be off, the name/address could be wrong, or a firewall in between could be dropping the probes. It does not prove the host is healthy, and it says nothing about your card being removed or your address changing.

items.prompt: You see `ifconfig` in an old tutorial, but it is missing on your fresh Linux system. What is the modern thing to do?
choices: Conclude the network is broken and reinstall the operating system
Use `ip` (the iproute2 tool), which is the modern replacement for the deprecated ifconfig
Edit the kernel to add ifconfig back
items.explanation: `ifconfig` is the old, deprecated tool and is often not installed anymore; the modern command is `ip` from the iproute2 toolkit (`ip addr`, `ip route`, and so on). Nothing is broken -- you just reach for the current tool instead of the legacy one.

items.prompt: What does the `-c3` option do in `ping -c3 8.8.8.8`?
choices: It makes ping send 3 probes and then stop, instead of running forever
It pings 3 different hosts at once
items.explanation: `-c3` means "count = 3": send three probes and then exit. Without a count, ping keeps going until you stop it. It is not a port number, not a delay, and not a way to target multiple hosts.

items.prompt: An interface named `wlan0` most likely represents what?
items.explanation: Interface names hint at the connection type: `wlan0` is a wireless (Wi-Fi) interface, while `eth0` is typically a wired Ethernet connection. Interfaces are network connections, not firewall rules or name-lookup services.

lessons.title: Connections and Ports: What Is Listening, What Is Connected
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Your machine isn't just sitting there with one address. At any moment some programs are *waiting* for visitors (a web server waiting for browsers), and some are *in the middle of a conversation* with another machine (your browser talking to a website). The tool that shows both is `ss`. It answers two questions: *what is listening here?* and *what am I connected to right now?*

**Build.** First, two plain words. A **port** is a numbered slot on a machine that picks which service a connection is for. One machine, one IP address, but many ports, so different services don't collide. Think of the IP address as a building and ports as numbered doors: door 22 is the secure-login service (SSH), door 80 is a plain web server, door 443 is a secure web server. A **socket** is just one endpoint of a network conversation, identified by an address and a port.

A program that is **listening** has opened a door and is waiting for someone to connect, like a shop with its door propped open during business hours. A connection that is **established** is a conversation already in progress, like a phone call already connected.

The command is `ss` (think "socket statistics"). The handful of letters you'll use most:

```bash
ss -ltn                    # listening (l), TCP (t), numeric (n): open doors
ss -tn state established   # conversations currently in progress
ss -tulpn                  # the everyday combo (see below)
```

That last one is worth memorizing: `t` = TCP, `u` = UDP, `l` = listening, `p` = show the program that owns each socket, `n` = numeric (don't slow down translating numbers to names). So `ss -tulpn` means "show me every listening TCP and UDP door, and tell me which program is behind each one." That last part is the key to a common task: *something is using a port, what is it?* A close relative does the same job from the program's side:

```bash
lsof -i :8080     # which program is using port 8080 (lsof = list open files)
```

Linux treats a network socket as a kind of open file, which is why a tool named "list open files" can answer network questions.

One note: `ss` is the modern replacement for an older command called `netstat`. If you see `netstat` in old guides, `ss` does the same job and is what's installed today.

**Recap.** A port is a numbered door; *listening* means a program is waiting at one, *established* means a conversation is in progress. `ss -tulpn` shows what's listening and which program owns it; `lsof -i :PORT` finds the program on a given port. `ss` is today's tool, replacing `netstat`.

items.prompt: What is a network port, in plain terms?
choices: The physical socket where the Ethernet cable plugs in
A numbered slot on a machine that selects which service a connection is for
Another name for the machine's IP address
The speed of the link in megabits per second
items.explanation: A port is a number that distinguishes services on the same machine, like numbered doors on one building (22 = SSH, 80 = web). It is not the physical cable jack, not the IP address itself, and not a measure of speed.

items.prompt: A socket is in the LISTEN state. What does that mean?
choices: A program has opened that port and is waiting for someone to connect
A conversation with a remote machine is already in progress
The port has been permanently blocked by the firewall
The machine is downloading a file from that port
items.explanation: LISTEN means a program has a door open and is waiting to ACCEPT new connections on that port, like a shop open for business. An in-progress conversation is the ESTABLISHED state, and blocking is a firewall matter, not a socket state.

items.prompt: Which command lists what is listening AND tells you the program behind each socket?
items.explanation: `ss -tulpn` shows listening TCP and UDP sockets (`t u l`), as numbers (`n`), with the owning program (`p`). `ip route` shows your gateway, `ping` tests reachability, and `ip addr` shows your address -- none of them lists listening sockets and their programs.

items.prompt: In `ss -tulpn`, what does the `p` stand for?
choices: show the program (process) that owns each socket
items.explanation: `p` asks ss to show the process/program owning each socket -- exactly what you need to answer "what is using this port?" The other letters are `t` (TCP), `u` (UDP), `l` (listening), and `n` (numeric). It has nothing to do with pinging, proxies, or address privacy.

items.prompt: You need to know which program is using port 8080. Which command answers that directly?
items.explanation: `lsof -i :8080` lists the program holding port 8080 (lsof = list open files, and Linux treats a socket as an open file). `ip link` shows interface state, `ping` needs a host not a bare port, and `ip route get` expects an address -- none identifies the program on a port.

items.prompt: Why can a tool called `lsof` ("list open files") answer questions about network connections?
choices: Because Linux treats a network socket as a kind of open file
Because lsof secretly runs ss underneath
Because every connection writes a log file to disk
Because files and ports are the same number
items.explanation: In Linux a network socket is represented as an open file held by a process, so a file-listing tool can report on sockets too (the `-i` option filters to network ones). It does not require ss, does not depend on disk log files, and files and ports are unrelated numbers.

items.prompt: Which command is the modern replacement for the older `netstat`?
items.explanation: `ss` replaces `netstat` for viewing sockets and connections and is what ships on current systems. `ifconfig`, `route`, and `arp` are the older address/route/neighbor tools (now handled by the `ip` command), not socket viewers.

items.prompt: A socket shows the ESTABLISHED state with a peer address of 140.82.112.3:443. What does this tell you?
choices: Your machine is waiting for someone to connect on port 443
Your machine has an active conversation in progress with that remote peer
Port 443 is blocked by the firewall
items.explanation: ESTABLISHED means an active connection is currently in progress between you and that peer (here, a secure web server on port 443). Waiting for connections is the LISTEN state, and an established connection is the opposite of blocked or down.

items.prompt: In the everyday picture used here, an IP address is the building and a port is the...
choices: numbered door that selects which service you reach
items.explanation: The analogy is: one IP address (the building) with many numbered ports (doors), each leading to a different service. Ports are not a street (that is more like the network) or a key, and they are certainly not a color.

lessons.title: Names to Numbers: How Linux Resolves a Hostname
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You type `example.com`, but the network only understands numbers like `93.184.216.34`. Something has to turn the friendly name into the number. That lookup is called **name resolution** (or just **DNS**), and on Linux it follows a specific path through a couple of files. Knowing that path is the difference between fixing a "can't find the server" problem and guessing.

**Build.** **DNS** stands for *Domain Name System*: the internet's phone book that maps names to IP addresses. When a program needs the number for a name, Linux checks sources in order:

1. **`/etc/hosts`** — a small local file you can edit, listing name-to-IP entries by hand. Like a personal speed-dial list you check before the public phone book. An entry looks like `93.184.216.34  example.com`. This is handy for testing or for naming machines on your own network.
2. **A DNS server** — if the name isn't in that local file, Linux asks a DNS server (the public phone book) over the network. Which server to ask is configured in **`/etc/resolv.conf`** (a `nameserver` line gives the server's IP).

So the friendly mental model is: *check my personal list first, then call the phone book.* The order itself is set in a file called `/etc/nsswitch.conf`, on its `hosts:` line (commonly `files dns`, meaning files first, then DNS) — you rarely change it, but that's where the order lives.

To test resolution the honest way — the same way normal programs do, including the `/etc/hosts` shortcut — use `getent`:

```bash
getent hosts example.com    # follows the real path: /etc/hosts then DNS
```

There are also dedicated DNS query tools, `dig` and `nslookup`:

```bash
dig example.com             # asks a DNS server directly
```

Here's the catch that trips beginners: `dig` talks straight to a DNS server and **skips `/etc/hosts`**. So a name you added to `/etc/hosts` will work in your browser but appear "missing" in `dig`. That's not a bug — `dig` is only testing the DNS-server step. Use `getent hosts` when you want to see what your programs actually see.

One modern wrinkle: many systems run a small local helper called *systemd-resolved*, and there `/etc/resolv.conf` is a managed file pointing at a local address (`127.0.0.53`). On those systems you inspect the real settings with `resolvectl status`. The point for now: don't be surprised if `/etc/resolv.conf` looks indirect.

**Recap.** A name is turned into a number by checking `/etc/hosts` first, then asking a DNS server listed in `/etc/resolv.conf`. Test the real path with `getent hosts`; remember `dig` queries DNS directly and ignores `/etc/hosts`.

items.prompt: What does DNS do?
choices: Maps friendly names (like example.com) to IP addresses
Decides which port a service listens on
items.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) is the internet's phone book: it turns names into the IP numbers the network actually uses. It is not encryption, not port assignment, and not routing/forwarding -- those are separate jobs.

items.prompt: When resolving a name, which source does Linux normally check before asking a DNS server?
items.explanation: With the common `files dns` order, Linux checks the local `/etc/hosts` file first, then falls back to a DNS server. `/etc/resolv.conf` lists which DNS server to ask, `/etc/fstab` is about mounting disks, and `/etc/passwd` is about user accounts.

items.prompt: Which file tells Linux which DNS server to ask over the network?
items.explanation: `/etc/resolv.conf` holds the `nameserver` line(s) naming the DNS server to query. `/etc/hosts` is the static local list, `/etc/nsswitch.conf` sets the order of sources, and `/etc/hostname` just stores this machine's own name.

items.prompt: You added `93.184.216.34  myserver` to `/etc/hosts`. The browser reaches `myserver`, but `dig myserver` says it cannot find it. Why?
choices: /etc/hosts is corrupt and must be deleted
dig queries a DNS server directly and does not read /etc/hosts
The entry needs a reboot to take effect
dig only works for real public domains
items.explanation: `dig` is a pure DNS-server query tool and deliberately ignores `/etc/hosts`, so a hand-added local entry won't appear there even though programs (and your browser) honor it. The file isn't corrupt and no reboot is needed -- use `getent hosts myserver` to see the real result.

items.prompt: Which command tests name resolution the same way normal programs do, including the /etc/hosts shortcut?
items.explanation: `getent hosts` walks the real resolver path (files then DNS, per nsswitch), so it reflects what applications actually see -- including `/etc/hosts`. `dig` skips `/etc/hosts`, `ping` to a literal IP tests reachability not name lookup, and `ss` shows sockets.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of an entry in /etc/hosts like `127.0.0.1  mylaptop`?
choices: It blocks that name in the firewall
It is a hand-written name-to-IP mapping checked before DNS
It sets the machine's default gateway
It opens a listening port for that name
items.explanation: An `/etc/hosts` line is a static, local name-to-IP mapping that the resolver checks before querying DNS -- useful for testing or naming local machines. It has nothing to do with firewall blocking, gateways, or opening ports.

items.prompt: On a system using systemd-resolved, you look at /etc/resolv.conf and it just points at 127.0.0.53. What is going on?
choices: DNS is broken because that is not a real server
It is a managed stub pointing at the local resolver; check the real settings with resolvectl status
Someone set your DNS server to your own laptop by mistake
items.explanation: With systemd-resolved, `/etc/resolv.conf` is a managed file pointing at the local resolver stub on 127.0.0.53; the actual upstream DNS servers are shown by `resolvectl status`. It is normal, not a misconfiguration, and 127.0.0.53 is a local resolver address, not a gateway.

items.prompt: In the analogy used here, /etc/hosts is like your personal speed-dial list and DNS is like...
choices: the public phone book you consult if the name is not on your list
items.explanation: The picture is: check your personal speed-dial (/etc/hosts) first, then call the public phone book (DNS) for anything not on it. Billing, busy signals, and ringtones don't map to any step in name resolution.

items.prompt: Which file decides the ORDER of sources (for example, files before DNS) for name lookups?
items.explanation: `/etc/nsswitch.conf` sets the lookup order on its `hosts:` line (commonly `files dns`). `/etc/resolv.conf` names the DNS server, `/etc/hosts` is the static list itself, and `/etc/services` just maps service names to port numbers.

lessons.title: The Firewall and the Router: Controlling What Passes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** So far you've been *looking* at the network. Now you *control* it. Two related powers: a **firewall** decides which incoming connections are allowed in (a reception desk that turns most visitors away), and **forwarding** lets one Linux machine pass traffic between two networks (a mailroom that relays letters from one wing to another). Both are conceptual here — the goal is to recognize what they do, not to memorize every rule.

**Build.** A **firewall** is a set of rules that decides, packet by packet, what may pass. The everyday picture is a building's reception desk: a visitor for a meeting (allowed) gets a badge; everyone else is turned away. The modern Linux firewall tool is **nftables**, run with the `nft` command. Its rules are organized as *tables* that hold *chains* that hold *rules*; a chain checks incoming packets top to bottom and each rule says `accept` or `drop`.

The most common safe pattern is **default-deny**: turn everyone away unless a rule explicitly lets them in. A firewall that allows secure login (SSH, port 22) and drops everything else inbound reads like this:

```bash
nft add table inet filter
nft 'add chain inet filter input { type filter hook input priority 0; policy drop; }'
nft add rule inet filter input ct state established accept   # let replies back in
nft add rule inet filter input iif lo accept                 # allow the machine to talk to itself
nft add rule inet filter input tcp dport 22 accept           # allow SSH
nft list ruleset                                             # show all rules
```

Read it plainly: the chain's *policy* is `drop` (turn everyone away by default); then we *accept* replies to connections we started, *accept* the machine talking to itself, and *accept* new SSH. Anything else falls through to the drop. `accept` lets a packet pass; `drop` silently discards it. As with the other tools, `nft` (nftables) is the modern replacement for the older `iptables`.

A close cousin of firewalling is **NAT** (*Network Address Translation*): rewriting addresses so a whole group of machines can share one public address. It's how your home router lets many devices reach the internet through a single address — just know the idea for now.

The second power is making Linux a **router**. Normally a Linux machine keeps to itself: it will *not* pass traffic from one network to another. You enable that with a single switch:

```bash
sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1    # allow this machine to forward packets between interfaces
```

With two network cards on two networks and that switch on, the machine relays packets between them — it has become a router (the mailroom is now open).

**Recap.** A firewall allows or denies traffic; the safe default-deny pattern accepts only what you list (like SSH) and drops the rest, using the modern `nft` (replacing `iptables`). Turning on `ip_forward` lets one Linux box route traffic between networks. NAT shares one address among many machines.

items.prompt: What does a firewall do?
choices: Decides, packet by packet, which network traffic is allowed to pass
items.explanation: A firewall is a set of rules that allows or blocks traffic -- like a reception desk admitting some visitors and turning others away. Turning names into numbers is DNS, and a firewall neither speeds up links nor stores files.

items.prompt: Which is the modern Linux firewall tool that replaces the older iptables?
items.explanation: nftables, used through the `nft` command, is the modern firewall front end that replaces iptables. `ifconfig` and `netstat` are older networking tools for addresses and sockets, and `ping` is a reachability test -- none is a firewall.

items.prompt: You want a firewall that allows SSH and blocks all other new inbound connections. Which approach is the safe one?
choices: Set the policy to accept, then add one rule dropping port 22
Set the policy to drop (deny by default), then add accept rules for replies, loopback, and SSH
Accept everything and hope the service is secure
Drop port 22 and accept everything else
items.explanation: The safe "default-deny" pattern sets the chain policy to drop and then explicitly accepts only what you want (established replies, the loopback interface, and new SSH); everything else falls through to the drop. Accepting by default, or dropping the very port you want open, gets it backwards.

items.prompt: In an nftables rule, what is the difference between `accept` and `drop`?
choices: accept lets the packet pass; drop silently discards it
accept logs the packet; drop emails an alert
accept is for TCP only; drop is for UDP only
They are two words for the same action
items.explanation: `accept` allows the packet through, while `drop` quietly throws it away with no reply. They are opposite actions, not synonyms, and they are not tied to logging, alerts, or a specific protocol.

items.prompt: How are nftables rules organized?
choices: Files contain folders that contain links
Tables contain chains that contain rules
Ports contain sockets that contain packets
Users contain groups that contain passwords
items.explanation: The nftables hierarchy is tables -> chains -> rules: a table holds chains, a chain is attached to a point in the packet path and holds an ordered list of rules. The other options describe unrelated parts of the system.

items.prompt: A Linux machine has two network cards on two different networks but refuses to pass traffic between them, even with correct addresses. What is most likely missing?
choices: A second IP address on one card
items.explanation: By default a Linux host does not forward packets between interfaces; you must enable it with `net.ipv4.ip_forward=1` for the box to act as a router. Extra addresses, faster cables, or DNS entries do not control whether forwarding happens.

items.prompt: What is NAT (Network Address Translation) used for, in everyday terms?
choices: Letting many devices share one public IP address to reach the internet
items.explanation: NAT rewrites addresses so a group of machines (for example, all the devices in a home) can share a single public IP address -- exactly what a home router does. It is not encryption, not a port scan, and not name resolution (that is DNS).

items.prompt: In the default-deny firewall above, why include the rule `ct state established accept`?
choices: To block all replies for safety
To let replies to connections you started come back in, so normal traffic still works
To open every port at once
To turn the machine into a router
items.explanation: `ct state established accept` admits return traffic for connections the machine itself started, so things like web requests still get their replies even though new inbound connections are dropped. It does not block replies, open all ports, or enable routing.

items.prompt: Which command lets you see the full set of firewall rules currently loaded?
items.explanation: `nft list ruleset` prints all loaded nftables tables, chains, and rules. `ip addr` shows addresses, `getent hosts` tests name resolution, and `ping` tests reachability -- none of those displays firewall rules.

lessons.title: Isolated Networks and Watching the Wire: Namespaces, veth, and tcpdump
lessons.content:
**Hook.** How does a container (a lightweight, isolated package for running a program, like Docker) get its *own* network — its own address and rules — without a second computer? Linux can hand a process a private copy of the whole network stack. And when you want to *watch* the actual traffic going by to debug a problem, there's a tool that shows you each packet on the wire. These are the deeper tools; treat this lesson as a guided tour of the ideas.

**Build.** A **network namespace** is a private, isolated copy of a machine's network: its own interfaces, its own addresses, its own routing table, its own firewall rules. A normal machine has one; Linux can create extra ones. Picture a large office where each team gets a separate, walled-off mailroom that can't see the others. This is exactly the trick containers use — a container runs in its own network namespace, which is why it feels like it has its own little network.

An isolated namespace has no way to reach anything — so we add a **veth pair**, a *virtual network cable*. It comes as two connected ends: whatever goes in one end comes out the other. Put one end inside a namespace and leave the other outside, and you've wired the namespace to the rest of the machine, like running a cable between two rooms.

```bash
ip netns add lab                                  # create a namespace called lab
ip link add veth0 type veth peer name veth1       # make a virtual cable (two ends)
ip link set veth1 netns lab                        # push one end into the namespace
ip netns exec lab ip addr add 10.0.0.2/24 dev veth1
ip netns exec lab ip link set veth1 up             # bring the inside end up
ip netns exec lab ping -c1 10.0.0.1                # run a command inside the namespace
```

`ip netns exec lab <command>` means "run this command inside the lab namespace." A common beginner slip: moving a cable end into a namespace doesn't switch it on or give it an address — you must add an address and bring it `up` inside. To connect *many* namespaces at once, you attach their outside ends to a **bridge** (a virtual switch); that is the model Docker uses to put many containers on one shared network.

The second tool is **tcpdump**, which *captures* and prints packets as they pass an interface — like holding a microphone up to the wire to hear the conversation. It's the go-to when you need to *prove* what is (or isn't) actually being sent:

```bash
tcpdump -ni eth0 port 53     # show packets on eth0 involving port 53 (DNS), as numbers
```

`-n` keeps it numeric (no slow name lookups) and `-i eth0` picks the interface. You'll see one line per packet with its source, destination, and ports.

A reminder tying the whole topic together: the modern toolset is `ip`, `ss`, and `nft`, which replace the older `ifconfig`, `netstat`, and `iptables`. And the *protocols* these tools carry — what's actually inside the packets tcpdump shows you — are the subject of the separate Networking topic.

**Recap.** A network namespace is a private copy of the network stack (the basis of container networking); a veth pair is the virtual cable that connects it; `ip netns exec` runs commands inside one; a bridge joins many. `tcpdump` captures packets so you can see real traffic. Modern `ip`/`ss`/`nft` replace `ifconfig`/`netstat`/`iptables`.

items.prompt: What is a network namespace?
choices: A private, isolated copy of a machine's network stack (its own interfaces, addresses, routes, and firewall rules)
Another name for an IP address
A faster kind of network cable
items.explanation: A network namespace gives a process its own isolated network stack -- separate interfaces, addresses, routing table, and firewall -- which is how containers get their own networking. It is not a hostname list, not an address, and not a cable.

items.prompt: What is a veth pair?
choices: A pair of physical Ethernet ports on the motherboard
A virtual network cable with two connected ends; traffic into one end comes out the other
A backup copy of the routing table
items.explanation: A veth pair is a virtual cable created as two linked ends, used to connect a namespace to the outside (or to a bridge). It is not physical hardware, not a firewall, and not a routing-table copy.

items.prompt: How does a container relate to network namespaces?
choices: Containers do not use the network at all
A container runs in its own network namespace, which gives it its own isolated network
All containers share one global routing table with the host directly
A container is just another name for a veth pair
items.explanation: A container runs inside its own network namespace, which is exactly why it appears to have its own address and rules. Containers very much use the network; they do not share one global table by default, and a container is a process environment, not a cable.

items.prompt: What does `ip netns exec lab ping -c1 10.0.0.1` do?
choices: Runs the ping command inside the network namespace named lab
Creates a new veth pair named lab
Adds a firewall rule to lab
items.explanation: `ip netns exec lab <command>` runs the given command inside the `lab` namespace, so here it pings from within that isolated network. It does not delete the namespace, create a cable, or add firewall rules.

items.prompt: You moved one end of a veth pair into a namespace but cannot ping across it. What is the most common beginner mistake?
choices: The interface inside still has no IP address and/or was never brought up
You must reboot to register a veth
items.explanation: Moving a cable end into a namespace does not assign it an address or switch it on; you must add an IP and bring it `up` inside the namespace before traffic flows. veth ends are made for namespaces, ping is not permanently blocked, and no reboot is needed.

items.prompt: What does tcpdump do?
choices: Captures and displays packets as they pass an interface, so you can see real traffic
items.explanation: tcpdump captures live packets on an interface and prints them, like holding a microphone to the wire -- ideal for proving what is actually being sent. Blocking is a firewall's job, address assignment is `ip addr`, and name lookups are DNS.

items.prompt: In `tcpdump -ni eth0 port 53`, what does the `-i eth0` part do?
choices: Sets the number of packets to capture
Chooses which interface to capture on
Saves the capture to a file
items.explanation: `-i eth0` selects the interface to listen on. The count is a separate option, `port 53` is the filter (here DNS traffic), and saving to a file would use a different option (`-w`).

items.prompt: To connect many namespaces together on one shared virtual network, you attach their outside ends to a...
items.explanation: A Linux bridge acts as a virtual switch; attaching each namespace's outside veth end to it puts them on one shared network -- the model Docker uses for many containers. A DNS server, a ping, or a drop rule does not join namespaces together.

items.prompt: Which set correctly pairs each modern tool with the older one it replaces?
choices: ip replaces ping; ss replaces dig; nft replaces tcpdump
ip replaces ifconfig; ss replaces netstat; nft replaces iptables
ip replaces iptables; ss replaces ifconfig; nft replaces netstat
ip replaces netstat; ss replaces iptables; nft replaces ifconfig
items.explanation: The modern toolkit maps cleanly: `ip` (addresses/links/routes) replaces `ifconfig`, `ss` (sockets) replaces `netstat`, and `nft` (firewall) replaces `iptables`. ping, dig, and tcpdump are separate tools that were not replaced by these.

items.prompt: This Linux topic gives you the tools (interfaces, sockets, routing, firewall). Where is the deep theory of the protocols inside the packets (TCP/IP and the rest) covered?
choices: It is the same thing as DNS
items.explanation: This module is the Linux-side companion: it teaches the commands to see and control networking. The protocol theory (TCP/IP, routing protocols, packet formats) is the subject of the separate Networking topic. It is broader than the tcpdump manual and distinct from DNS.
